setup.title: Initial setup
setup.tokenPlaceholder: Paste the setup token from the server console

setupPage.title: Setup
setupPage.subtitle: Onboarding for new installs.
setupPage.freshInstallBadge: Fresh install — no defaults, frontends, ACLs, backends, or certs yet
setupPage.emptyAlert: patchpanel is still empty. The wizard collects a Let's Encrypt account, the first defaults block, frontend, ACL + use-backend rule, backend, and covering certificate — enough to render a valid haproxy.cfg.

setupAdmin.tokenExistsUserRegistered: A setup token exists but at least one user is already registered.
setupAdmin.tokenConsumed: The setup token has been consumed. If you need to recover access, use the `patchpanel user-add` or `patchpanel user-reset` CLI on the host.
setupAdmin.tokenInstructionsPrefix: Paste the one-time setup token from the install banner, or read it off the host with
setupAdmin.tokenInstructionsSuffix: The token is consumed on success and the file is deleted.
setupAdmin.tokenHelp: Prefilled when you opened this page from the banner URL; paste it manually otherwise.
setupAdmin.usernameHelp: Lowercase letters/digits/dot/underscore/hyphen, starting with a letter (2-32 chars).

changePassword.title: Change password
changePassword.successMessage: Password updated. Other sessions for this user have been invalidated.

profile.title: Profile
profile.ingressNote: Authenticated upstream by Home Assistant ingress — local password change and logout are not available in this mode.

apiTokens.title: API tokens
apiTokens.name: Token name
apiTokens.copyWarning: Copy this token now — you won't see it again.
apiTokens.nameHelp: Human-readable label. Letters, digits, space, dot, underscore, hyphen.
apiTokens.revokeConfirmBodySuffix: Any scripts using it will start receiving 401 responses immediately. This cannot be undone.
apiTokens.subtitleSuffix: The secret is shown once at creation and cannot be retrieved later — revoke and re-issue if lost.

authProvider.idFormatError: id must match a-z, 0-9, _, - (starting with a letter)
authProvider.autheliaWizardSuggestion: Need patchpanel to generate the backend, host ACL, and portal use-backend rule for you? The Authelia setup wizard creates them all in one shot.
authProvider.typeHelp: Type cannot change after creation. Delete and recreate to switch types.

upstream.intro: This wizard creates 4 state entities in one shot: a Backend pointing at your Authelia server, an ACL matching the portal hostname, a use-backend Rule on the chosen HTTPS frontend that routes browser portal traffic to that backend, and the AuthProvider entity itself (referencing the new backend by id). It also registers the bundled auth-request.lua plugin under globalSettings.luaPlugins if it isn't already there. All editable later as first-class state.
upstream.versionHelp: Modern emits X-Forwarded-* before the auth probe; legacy emits a single X-Original-URL. Auto-fills the path + redirect template below.
upstream.serverAddressHelp: host:port of your Authelia instance. Same backend serves both the lua auth probe and the browser portal.
upstream.endpointPathHelp: Auto-filled from the version selector; override only if your Authelia is reverse-mounted at a non-default path.

routing.hostHelp: The hostname users hit to reach the Authelia web portal. Make sure it's on the covering certificate's SAN list.
routing.frontendHelp: The use-backend rule is inserted at the top of this frontend's rulePhases.httpRequest, so portal traffic matches before any other route on that hostname.

review.gatingHintPrefix: After save, gating other routes is one more step: add an
review.gatingHintMiddle: rule on the gated frontend with
review.gatingHintSuffix: and a condition matching the host you want protected. Do that from the Rules tab.

welcome.title: Set up your first working route
welcome.body1: This wizard walks through six short steps to produce an end-to-end working state — a defaults block, frontend, backend, ACL + use-backend rule, TLS provider, and covering cert. The output runs through the standard apply pipeline (schema validate → render → haproxy -c → atomic write → master-socket reload).
welcome.body2: Re-running this on an already-configured install adds new entities with collision-safe ids; existing ones are left alone.

identity.cloudflareTokenHelpPrefix: Create a scoped API token in the Cloudflare dashboard with
identity.cloudflareTokenHelpSuffix: on the zone(s) you want to issue certs for. patchpanel writes the token to a mode-600 file in the credentials directory; it never leaves the addon.

provider.cloudflareDescription: Best for wildcards and when port 80 isn't reachable. Requires a Cloudflare API token (collected below).
provider.http01Description: Works with any DNS host. Requires port 80 reachable from the public Internet.

review.applyHintStoresToken: and stores the Cloudflare API token
review.applyHintRenewPrefix: The cert isn't issued automatically — open the Certificates tab and click
